studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse is developing a plan of care for a client with a psychotic disorder who is experiencing altered thoughts that include the belief food is being poisoned. Which strategy should the nurse plan to implement that will encourage the client to discuss feelings?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Open-ended questions and silence are strategies used to encourage clients to discuss their feelings. None of the remaining options attempt to encourage communication with the client.

Client Needs: Psychosocial Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Generate Solutions
Cognitive Ability: Applying
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Phobias
Integrated Process: Nursing Process/Planning
Priority Concepts: Psychosis, Clinical Judgment
Strategies: Subject

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, strategies to encourage the client to discuss feelings. Eliminate the incorrect options because they do not support client expression of feelings. Additionally, these options are not client-focused. Focusing on the client's feelings will direct you to the correct option.

optionList.content: Declines an invitation to walk around the park
Never takes an elevator but rather climbs the stairs
Always turns on the overhead light before entering a darkened room
Refuses to engage in conversations when in the presence of more than two to three people\

studentQuestionArray.content: Which behavior would the nurse anticipate in a client diagnosed with nyctophobia?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Nyctophobia is a fear of darkness. Social phobias are characterized by severe anxiety or fear provoked by exposure to a social situation or a performance situation. Fear of public speaking is the most common social phobia. Agoraphobia is a fear of open spaces. Claustrophobia is a fear of closed places.

Client Needs: Psychosocial Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Recognize Cues
Cognitive Ability: Analyzing
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Phobias
Integrated Process: Nursing Process/Assessment
Priority Concepts: Psychosis, Clinical Judgment
Strategies: Subject

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, nyctophobia. It is necessary to understand the description of nyctophobia. Recalling that this is a fear of darkness will direct you to the correct option.

"I went to the movies with my family and stayed through the whole film by sitting in a seat along the aisle."

studentQuestionArray.content: The home health nurse visits an agoraphobic client who experiences panic attacks. Which statement by the client would indicate a therapeutic response to behavioral and pharmacological treatment?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Generalizing fears to a specific place or situation is the hallmark of agoraphobia. Improvement is observed when the client is able to demonstrate appropriate coping behaviors for anxiety reduction. Taking extra anxiety medication would not indicate improvement. "Clock-watching" with regard to the medication schedule is also not a sign that the client is responding well to the treatment.

Client Needs: Physiological Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Evaluate Outcomes
Cognitive Ability: Evaluating
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Phobias
Integrated Process: Nursing Process/Evaluation
Priority Concepts: Stress and Coping, Anxiety
Strategies: Comparable or Alike Options, Subject

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, therapeutic response to treatment and appropriate coping. The correct option is the only one that demonstrates the client's use of an appropriate coping mechanism. Also note that the remaining options are comparable or alike in that the client relies on antianxiety medications.

optionList.content: Daily medication therapy
Involvement with a support group
Intense stress management training
Short exposure to the phobic object
Daily medication therapy
Involvement with a support group
Intense stress management training
Short exposure to the phobic object

studentQuestionArray.content: A client's phobia is being treated with systematic desensitization. Which modality is the focus of this therapy?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Systematic desensitization is a form of therapy in which the client is introduced to short periods of exposure to the phobic object while in a relaxed state. Gradually, over a period of time, exposure is increased until the anxiety about or fear of the object or situation has ceased. Medication is associated with pharmacological therapy. While stress management techniques and self-help groups may be helpful, neither is the basis of this therapy.

Client Needs: Psychosocial Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Take Action
Cognitive Ability: Applying
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Phobias
Integrated Process: Nursing Process/Implementation
Priority Concepts: Anxiety, Psychosis
Strategies: Subject

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, components of systematic desensitization. Specific knowledge regarding this form of therapy is required to answer this question. Also, thinking about the definition of desensitization will direct you to the correct option.

optionList.content: Having the client confront the anxiety-provoking stimulus and providing support during the episode
Instructing the client to do what the client fears and, if possible, to exaggerate the outcome of this exposure to the point of humor
Presenting the anxiety-provoking stimulus without any preparation of the client and having him or her remain exposed until the anxiety subsides
Using progressive relaxation toward the client's individual anxiety hierarchy, increasing the level of difficulty, and pairing relaxation with the gradual exposure to reduce his or her anxiety
Having the client confront the anxiety-provoking stimulus and providing support during the episode

studentQuestionArray.content: The psychiatric home care nurse visits a client diagnosed with a phobia that triggers panic attacks. When teaching the client to use paradoxical intention, which intervention will the nurse demonstrate?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
In cognitive-behavioral therapy, the client with a phobia who experiences panic attacks will be treated with a combination of cognitive restructuring, exposure therapy, and paradoxical intention. In paradoxical intention the client is instructed to do what he fears and, if possible, to exaggerate it to the point of humor. When this occurs, the client is taught to prevent the anxiety by a variety of coping mechanisms. This assists the client to regain an internal locus of control or feeling of empowerment and to master response to the anxiety-provoking issue or situation. Other options describe in vivo therapy, flooding, and systematic desensitization.

Client Needs: Psychosocial Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Take Action
Cognitive Ability: Applying
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Phobias
Integrated Process: Teaching and Learning
Priority Concepts: Stress and Coping, Anxiety
Strategies: Subject

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, paradoxical intention and care of the client with a phobia experiencing panic attacks. Note the relationship between the word intention in the question and to do what the client fears in the correct option.

optionList.content: Sad and tearful
Suspicious and hostile
Frightened and delusional
Rigidness in thought and inflexibility
Sad and tearful
Suspicious and hostile
Frightened and delusional
Rigidness in thought and inflexibility

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse preparing to admit a client with a diagnosis of obsessive-compulsive disorder to the mental health unit would expect to note which behaviors in the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Rigid and inflexible behaviors are characteristic of the client with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). Clients with this disorder usually are not hostile unless they are prevented from engaging in the obsession or compulsion because this behavior is what decreases the anxiety. None of the other options are associated with OCD.

Client Needs: Psychosocial Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Recognize Cues
Cognitive Ability: Analyzing
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
Integrated Process: Nursing Process/Assessment
Priority Concepts: Anxiety, Mood and Affect
Strategies: Subject


Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, behaviors in OCD. Think about the psychopathology associated with this disorder to answer correctly. Remember that rigid and inflexible behaviors are characteristic of the client with obsessive-compulsive disorder.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client with a diagnosis of agoraphobia. Which statement made by the client would support this diagnosis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Agoraphobia is a fear of leaving the house and experiencing panic attacks when doing so. The remaining options describe obsessive-compulsive behavior, claustrophobia, and a social phobia.

Client Needs: Psychosocial Integrity
Clinical Judgment/Cognitive Skill(s): Recognize Cues
Cognitive Ability: Analyzing
Content Area: Mental Health
Health Problem: Mental Health: Phobias
Integrated Process: Nursing Process/Assessment
Priority Concepts: Anxiety, Psychosis
Strategies: Subject

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, agoraphobia. Recalling the definition of this word will direct you to the correct option. Remember that agoraphobia is a fear of leaving the house and experiencing panic attacks when doing so.
